skill.name: python-packaging
skill.description: Guides users through modern Python package structure, pyproject.toml metadata, build workflows, CLI entry points, and PyPI publishing steps.
skill.summary: Build, test, and publish Python packages with modern packaging practices.
skill.author: ActiveInferenceInstitute

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell, URL, credential, and weak-crypto patterns, but review shows they are mostly fenced documentation examples for Python packaging. The real residual risk is operational: the skill teaches publishing commands, custom package indexes, and token configuration in ~/.pypirc, so users need a marketplace warning about credentials and release actions.

medium_findings.title: Credential Configuration Guidance for PyPI Tokens
medium_findings.description: The skill shows how to create ~/.pypirc and place PyPI API token placeholders in password fields. This is legitimate packaging documentation, but it can lead agents or users to write sensitive tokens into a hidden home-directory file if handled without explicit consent and file-permission checks.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly documents ~/.pypirc and token password fields. The tokens are placeholders, so this is not exfiltration, but it is a real credential-handling risk.
medium_findings.remediation: Warn users before editing credential files, prefer trusted secret managers or CI secrets, and avoid storing real tokens in generated examples.

medium_findings.title: Package Publishing Commands Can Perform External Release Actions
medium_findings.description: The skill includes twine upload commands for TestPyPI, PyPI, and automated CI publishing. These commands are expected for a packaging skill, but they can publish artifacts or send credentials to package indexes if executed without user confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are direct publishing workflows and include token-backed CI upload. They are not malicious, but the side effects are significant enough to require caution.
medium_findings.remediation: Require explicit user approval before publishing, test on TestPyPI first, and verify package names, versions, and target repository URLs.

medium_findings.title: Custom Package Index Examples Require Dependency Trust Checks
medium_findings.description: The skill documents installing from TestPyPI and private package indexes. These URLs are normal for packaging work, but alternate indexes can introduce dependency confusion or untrusted package sources if copied without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples intentionally use non-default indexes. The domains are placeholders or standard test infrastructure, so the risk is contextual rather than evidence of malicious behavior.
medium_findings.remediation: Tell users to verify index ownership, pin expected packages, and avoid mixing untrusted extra indexes with production installs.

low_findings.title: External Command Alerts Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The external command findings occur inside Markdown code fences and usage snippets such as pip install, python -m build, twine check, and test commands. No executable helper script or automatic command runner is present in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-number review shows command text inside fenced examples. There is no evidence that the skill executes commands automatically.
low_findings.remediation: Keep command examples clearly fenced and require user confirmation before running packaging commands.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Alerts Are Packaging References or Placeholders
low_findings.description: The URL findings are project metadata examples, documentation badge URLs, package index examples, or links to official packaging resources. No evidence found of hidden callbacks or data exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs match visible documentation and packaging examples. Their context is descriptive, not covert network access.
low_findings.remediation: Ask users to replace placeholder URLs with repositories and documentation sites they control.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography blocker alerts map to ordinary packaging text such as Markdown file names, descriptions, version examples, or badge text. No cryptographic implementation or insecure hash use was found in the reviewed skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain prose or sample metadata, not crypto code. The static rule appears to match short substrings in documentation.
low_findings.remediation: No security change required; keep examples simple and avoid ambiguous token names where practical.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alert Is a Dependency Version Example
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance finding at the cited line is an exact dependency version example for numpy. No host probing, environment discovery, or system inventory behavior is present there.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is inside a pyproject dependency example. It does not inspect the local system or collect host information.
low_findings.remediation: No remediation needed for reconnaissance; dependency pinning guidance can mention update and vulnerability review tradeoffs.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive Credential File Mentioned
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation references ~/.pypirc, a hidden file that may store package publishing credentials. This is expected for PyPI workflows but should be treated as sensitive.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact sensitive file path and token fields are shown in the skill. The example uses placeholders, reducing but not eliminating risk.
dangerous_patterns.remediation: Use token placeholders only, avoid printing real tokens, and confirm before writing or reading ~/.pypirc.

dangerous_patterns.title: Networked Package Install and Upload Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes package install and upload examples that contact public, test, or private package indexes. These commands have expected network side effects.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network commands are explicit and central to package publishing. They are legitimate examples rather than hidden behavior.
dangerous_patterns.remediation: Confirm target indexes and package names before execution, and prefer dry-run or test repository workflows where available.

content.value_statement: Python packaging has many standards, files, and release steps that are easy to mix up. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical guidance for building, testing, and publishing packages.
actual_capabilities: Explains src-layout and flat-layout project structures for Python packages.
Provides pyproject.toml examples for setuptools, dynamic versions, dependencies, and metadata.
Shows CLI entry point patterns using Click and argparse.
Guides local build, wheel checks, TestPyPI testing, and PyPI publishing workflows.
Covers package data, namespace packages, C extension examples, README templates, and release checklists.
limitations: It is a documentation-only skill and does not include scripts or automated validators.
Examples use placeholder names, URLs, and tokens that users must replace carefully.
It does not audit package dependencies for vulnerabilities or license issues.
Publishing steps can affect real package indexes and require explicit user confirmation.

use_cases.title: Create a New Library Package
use_cases.description: Use the skill to design a src-layout project, define package metadata, and prepare wheel and source distributions.

use_cases.title: Package a Command-Line Tool
use_cases.description: Use the CLI entry point patterns to expose commands through installed console scripts.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Release Workflow
use_cases.description: Use the build, TestPyPI, twine, and GitHub Actions guidance to plan a controlled package release.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Package
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the python-packaging skill to outline a minimal src-layout package for my project. Include the files I need and explain each file briefly.

prompt_templates.title: Write Package Metadata
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the python-packaging skill to draft pyproject.toml guidance for a package named [package name]. Include dependencies, optional dev tools, classifiers, and project URLs.

prompt_templates.title: Add a CLI Entry Point
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the python-packaging skill to help package my CLI tool. Recommend an entry point pattern and describe how users will install and run it.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Safe Release
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the python-packaging skill to create a release checklist for PyPI. Include build checks, TestPyPI validation, token handling, and final publishing confirmation.

output_examples.input: I need to package a small Python utility for PyPI.
output: A list of required packaging files and metadata decisions.
A short build and TestPyPI validation sequence.

output_examples.input: I want my package to install a terminal command.
output: Testing steps for editable installs and installed commands.
Warnings about credentials, target index, and version number.
Recommendations for README, license, metadata, and distribution checks.

best_practices: Use src-layout and pyproject.toml for new packages unless an existing project requires another pattern.
Test builds and installs in a clean environment before publishing to any package index.
Treat PyPI tokens and private index credentials as secrets, and confirm before writing credential files.
anti_patterns: Publishing directly to production PyPI before testing the built package on TestPyPI or in a clean environment.
Copying placeholder package names, repository URLs, or token values into a real release workflow.
Mixing public and private package indexes without checking dependency names and trusted sources.

faq.question: Does this skill run packaging commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides Markdown guidance and command examples. Users should approve any command before execution.

faq.question: Can it help with pyproject.toml?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for build systems, project metadata, dependencies, optional dependencies, and tool configuration.

faq.question: Does it support PyPI publishing?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains building distributions, checking artifacts, testing on TestPyPI, and publishing with twine.

faq.question: Is credential handling required?
faq.answer: Only for publishing workflows. Use placeholders in generated examples and protect real PyPI tokens as secrets.

faq.question: Can it package CLI tools?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows Click and argparse patterns and explains console script registration.

faq.question: Does it replace a security review?
faq.answer: No. It does not scan dependencies, validate licenses, or prove that a package is safe to publish.
